overview: The US has turned its firepower on Iran's infrastructure. In a sixth straight night of strikes, American forces hit five bridges in southern Iran plus a train station and power grid around the port city of Bandar Abbas — making good on President Trump's threat to bomb bridges and power plants unless Tehran returns to talks. Iran says seven were killed in the latest wave and warns the war 'will spread.' At sea, US Marines boarded a tanker running the port blockade. The one hopeful thread — Tehran's release of an American detainee — is now disputed, even as Iran's strongest regional card wobbles: Lebanon is moving to end Hezbollah's armed presence.

keyPoints.text: US strikes expanded to five bridges in southern Iran plus a Bandar Abbas train station and power grid; Iran reports seven killed and warns the war 'will spread'.
US Marines boarded the tanker M/T Wen Yao in the Gulf of Oman — the blockade escalates from disabling ships with missiles to physical boarding.
Yesterday's off-ramp narrows: Reuters reports the release of US detainee Dena Karari is now 'in dispute' as both sides step up attacks.
Lebanon's government moves to 'end Hezbollah's military presence', potentially unwinding Iran's most powerful regional front.
IEA chief Fatih Birol warns the Hormuz crisis threatens global energy security; US regular gas nears $4 a gallon and diesel tops $5.
Falling debris from intercepted Iranian strikes sparked fires on the Kuwait–Iraq border; Qatar rejected Israeli reports it agreed to join military action against Iran.

watch.text: Whether Trump's infrastructure bombing forces Tehran back to the table or triggers the wider war Iran is now warning of.
Iran's threat to halt all Middle East energy exports if US strikes on its infrastructure continue.
Lebanon's push to disarm Hezbollah — whether Beirut can assert a state monopoly on force without reigniting internal conflict.

stories.title: US bombs bridges and power grid as strike campaign hits infrastructure
stories.summary: In a sixth consecutive night of attacks, the US expanded its air campaign from military targets to Iranian infrastructure, striking five bridges in southern Iran and, according to Iranian state agency IRNA, a train station and the electricity grid around the port city of Bandar Abbas, causing power outages. The strikes make good on President Trump's warning that he would bomb Iran's bridges and power plants unless Tehran resumed talks. Iran said seven people were killed in the latest wave and warned that the war 'will spread'; Iranian sources alleged the strikes hit civilian infrastructure and caused casualties, claims the US has not confirmed.
stories.explainer: Deliberately striking bridges, rail and the power grid marks a shift from a campaign aimed at military targets — missiles, air defenses, naval sites — to strategic coercion, using pressure on civilian life to force a government back to the table. It is a high-risk escalation: infrastructure strikes raise the civilian toll, are hard to reverse, and historically tend to harden a population's resolve rather than break it, which is why Iran frames them as war crimes and vows the conflict will widen.

stories.title: US Marines board tanker as the blockade of Iran's ports tightens
stories.summary: US Marines boarded the tanker M/T Wen Yao in the Gulf of Oman, the US military said — a sharp escalation of the reimposed blockade, which until now had relied on disabling ships from the air, as when a US aircraft crippled an unladen tanker bound for the Kharg Island oil terminal with Hellfire missiles. Iran, which declared the Strait of Hormuz closed on 12 July, says the waterway will not return to its pre-war status. The head of the International Energy Agency, Fatih Birol, warned that the crisis threatens global energy security and that the world should be 'worried' if it does not improve.
stories.explainer: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves through the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint narrow enough that a handful of small islands sit astride its shipping lanes. Boarding a vessel is a heavier step than disabling one from the air: it puts American personnel on a foreign-flagged ship in contested water, creating direct opportunities for a firefight, hostage-taking, or a miscalculation that pulls other navies in — which is why each escalation of enforcement raises, rather than lowers, the odds of a wider clash.

stories.title: Off-ramp narrows as detainee release is thrown into dispute
stories.summary: The release of Dena Karari, a dual US-Iranian citizen detained since December 2024, which President Trump announced on 15 July as a possible opening, is now 'in dispute', Reuters reports, even as Iran and the US step up their attacks. Adding to the murk, Qatar rejected Israeli media reports that it had agreed to join military action against Iran, and Iran's foreign ministry insisted Tehran 'never welcomed war'. Cutting the other way, the family of Craig Foreman, a Briton jailed in Iran, said his sentence had been extended by two years for speaking to the media from prison.
stories.explainer: Detainee releases are usually the lowest-cost trust signal in US-Iran diplomacy, which is why a release turning contested matters: if the two sides cannot even agree on whether a hand-over happened, the thin channel of indirect, Qatar-mediated contacts has little to build on, and the gesture read yesterday as a path to climbdown loses its meaning.

stories.title: Lebanon moves to end Hezbollah's armed presence
stories.summary: Lebanon's foreign minister, Youssef Raggi, said the government had taken a decision to 'end Hezbollah's military presence', stressing that decisions on war and foreign policy are the 'exclusive prerogative of the Lebanese state'. The move to assert a state monopoly on force and disarm the Iran-backed group comes as Beirut tries to keep out of the widening US-Iran war and after the 2026 Lebanon war left more than a sixth of the country's population displaced.
stories.explainer: Hezbollah is the most powerful member of Iran's 'Axis of Resistance' — the network of allied militias Tehran uses to project force across the region — and its Lebanon front tied down Israeli forces earlier in the war. A Lebanese government push to disarm it would strip Iran of its strongest regional lever at the moment Tehran most needs to threaten escalation, though forcing the issue risks reopening Lebanon's own internal conflict.

stories.title: Energy shock spreads: IEA warning, $4 gas, fires in Kuwait
stories.summary: The economic shock of the reignited war is widening. IEA chief Fatih Birol warned that 'oil security is still a critical issue' and that the Hormuz crisis threatens global energy security. In the US, regular gasoline is nearing $4 a gallon and diesel has topped $5 — nearly a dollar and a quarter higher than a year ago — while oil, shipping and flights face renewed disruption. The war's reach spread further as falling debris from intercepted Iranian strikes sparked fires on the Kuwait–Iraq border, and analysts estimate the conflict's cost could run toward $100bn.
stories.explainer: Because Hormuz carries such a large share of seaborne oil, disruption there feeds through to pump prices worldwide within weeks — and every spillover, from a projectile landing in Kuwait to a stranded tanker, widens the circle of countries with a direct stake in how the war ends.

notes: Briefing synthesized from the day's wire feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-17T07:29Z). The manual RSS refresh was blocked by the proxy, but the scheduled workflow had already refreshed the feed this morning. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — casualty counts, the disputed detainee release, alleged strikes on civilian sites — are attributed to their sources.
